verbatim: The whole country was in the hands of a few persons, and if the tenants failed to pay their rent they were liable to be haled into slavery, and their children with them. All loans were secured upon the debtor’s person, a custom which prevailed until the time of Solon, who was the first to appear as the champion of the people.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a general legal custom in pre-Solonian Athens where failure to pay rent or debts resulted in the loss of personal freedom for the debtor and their family.

verbatim: The magistrates were elected according to qualifications of birth and wealth. At first they governed for life, but subsequently for terms of ten years.
This was the natural consequence of the facts that the Archons were elected under qualifications of birth and wealth, and that the Areopagus was composed of those who had served as Archons
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that holding high magisterial office was restricted to those meeting specific criteria of lineage (birth) and economic standing (wealth).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to vote in the Ekklesia
verbatim: The franchise was given to all who could furnish themselves with a military equipment. The nine Archons and the Treasurers were elected by this body from persons possessing an unencumbered property of not less than ten minas
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under Draco's constitution, political rights (the franchise) were tied to the ability to afford hoplite armor, representing a specific wealth threshold.

verbatim: The nine Archons and the Treasurers were elected by this body from persons possessing an unencumbered property of not less than ten minas, the less important officials from those who could furnish themselves with a military equipment, and the generals [Strategi] and commanders of the cavalry [Hipparchi] from those who could show an unencumbered property of not less than a hundred minas
extracted_rules.reasoning: Draco's ordinances established specific minimum wealth requirements (measured in minas) for different levels of political and military leadership.

verbatim: Both for this and for the other magistracies the lot was cast among those who were over thirty years of age; and no one might hold office twice until every one else had had his turn
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific age boundary (30 years) as a mandatory requirement for participating in the lottery for public offices.

verbatim: If any member of the Council failed to attend when there was a sitting of the Council or of the Assembly, he paid a fine, to the amount of three drachmas if he was a Pentacosiomedimnus, two if he was a Knight, and one if he was a Zeugites.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law imposed heavier financial burdens on members of higher property classes for failure to perform civic duties, linking resource loss to wealth status.

verbatim: As soon as he was at the head of affairs, Solon liberated the people once and for all, by prohibiting all loans on the security of the debtor’s person: and in addition he made laws by which he cancelled all debts, public and private.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Solon's reform (the Seisachtheia) established a new legal rule prohibiting the use of one's person as collateral, thereby protecting the group from being enslaved due to debt.

verbatim: He divided the population according to property into four classes, namely, Pentacosiomedimni, Knights, Zeugitae, and Thetes. The various magistracies, namely, the nine Archons, the Treasurers, the Commissioners for Public Contracts [Poletae], the Eleven, and the Exchequer Clerks [Colacretae], he assigned to the Pentacosiomedimni, the Knights, and the Zeugitae, giving offices to each class in proportion to the value of their rateable property. To those who ranked among the Thetes he gave nothing but a place in the Assembly and in the juries.

Those ranked as Zeugitae who made two hundred measures, liquid or solid; and the rest ranked as Thetes, and were not eligible for any office. Hence it is that even at the present day, when a candidate for any office is asked to what class he belongs, no one would think of saying that he belonged to the Thetes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly defines the property-based social classes and states that the lowest class, the Thetes, were legally excluded from holding specific named administrative and executive offices (magistracies).

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for the office of Treasurer
verbatim: A proof that Solon regulated the elections to office according to the property classes may be found in the law still in force with regard to the Treasurers, which enacts that they shall be chosen from the Pentacosiomedimni.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle identifies a specific law, still active in his own time, that restricts the pool of candidates for the role of Treasurer to the highest property class.

verbatim: Further, since he saw the state often engaged in internal disputes, while many of the citizens from sheer indifference accepted whatever might turn up, he made a law with express reference to such persons, enacting that any one who, in a time civil factions, did not take up arms with either party, should lose his rights as a citizen and cease to have any part in the state.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law mandates active participation in civil conflicts; failure to choose a side results in the loss of one's legal status as a citizen.

verbatim: There are three points in the constitution of Solon which appear to be its most democratic features: first and most important, the prohibition of loans on the security of the debtor’s person.
And those that here in vilest slavery crouched ‘neath a master’s frown, I set them free.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The reform explicitly banned the practice of using one's own body as collateral for loans, which previously allowed citizens to be sold into slavery.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to legal standing for others
verbatim: secondly, the right of every person who so willed to claim redress on behalf of any one to whom wrong was being done.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This rule allowed any citizen (not just the victim) to initiate legal action to seek justice for a wrong, expanding access to the legal system.

verbatim: thirdly, the institution of the appeal to the jury-courts; and it is to this last, they say, that the masses have owed their strength most of all, since, when the democracy is master of the voting-power, it is master of the constitution.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines the ability to appeal magistrate decisions to a jury of citizens as a core constitutional right granted to the public.

verbatim: After this it was agreed, as a compromise, to elect ten Archons, five from the Eupatridae, three from the Agroeci, and two from the Demiurgi, and they ruled for the year following Damasias.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the expulsion of Damasias, a formal rule was established allocating a specific number of Archon seats to specific social/hereditary classes (Eupatridae/Nobles, Agroeci/Farmers, Demiurgi/Artisans), granting the Eupatridae a majority of those reserved seats.

verbatim: A proof of this is seen in the fact that after the tyranny was overthrown a revision was made of the citizen-roll, on the ground that many persons were partaking in the franchise without having a right to it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic revision (diapsephismos) where individuals whose lineage was not considered 'pure' were stripped of their voting rights and status as citizens.

verbatim: Not only was he in every respect humane and mild and ready to forgive those who offended, but, in addition, he advanced money to the poorer people to help them in their labours, so that they might make their living by agriculture.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under Pisistratus, a general state policy was implemented to provide loans to the poor specifically to enable them to work in the countryside.

verbatim: At the same time his revenues were increased by the thorough cultivation of the country, since he imposed a tax of one tenth on all the produce.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The administration established a mandatory land tax requiring one-tenth of all agricultural produce to be paid to the state/tyrant.

verbatim: For the same reasons he instituted the local justices, and often made expeditions in person into the country to inspect it and to settle disputes between individuals, that they might not come into the city and neglect their farms.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The institution of 'local justices' (δικασταὶ κατὰ δήμους) provided a legal resource for people in the countryside, allowing them to settle disputes without traveling to the city.

verbatim: The law ran as follows: ‘These are the ancestral statutes of the Athenians; if any persons shall make an attempt to establish a tyranny, or if any person shall join in setting up a tyranny, he shall lose his civic rights, both himself and his whole house.’
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text quotes an ancestral law (atmia) that automatically strips any person and their family of all civic rights if they attempt to establish a tyranny.

verbatim: On this Cleisthenes retired from the country, and Cleomenes, entering Attica with a small force, expelled, as polluted, seven hundred Athenian families.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a general application of the religious/political concept of 'pollution' to expel a large group (700 families) rather than a single individual, showing that entire lineages could lose their right to reside in the territory based on ancestral status.

verbatim: All who lived in any given deme he declared fellow-demesmen, to the end that the new citizens might not be exposed by the habitual use of family names, but that men might be officially described by the names of their demes; and accordingly it is by the names of their demes that the Athenians speak of one another.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Cleisthenes established a rule where citizenship and official identity were tied to the deme (locality) rather than family names, specifically to protect and integrate new citizens into the franchise.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain family and clan religious rites
verbatim: On the other hand he allowed every one to retain his family and clan and religious rites according to ancestral custom.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Despite the administrative reforms into tribes and demes, a specific rule was maintained protecting the immaterial resource of ancestral religious and clan traditions for existing groups.

extracted_rules.rule: Protection of the state through ostracism
extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain in the city
verbatim: Thus for three years they continued to ostracize the friends of the tyrants, on whose account the law had been passed; but in the following year they began to remove others as well, including any one who seemed to be more powerful than was expedient.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Ostracism was a formal legal mechanism in Athens allowing the temporary expulsion of citizens who gained 'expedient' power, representing a loss of the right to reside in the city.

verbatim: it was laid down for the future that persons under sentence of ostracism must live between Geraestus and Scyllaeum, on pain of losing their civic rights irrevocably.
extracted_rules.reasoning: A specific legal amendment defined a geographic boundary for those ostracized; crossing this boundary resulted in the total and permanent loss of citizenship (civic rights).

verbatim: Out of the proceeds of the tributes and the taxes and the contributions of the allies more than twenty thousand persons were maintained. There were 6,000 jurymen, 1,600 bowmen, 1,200 Knights, 500 members of the Council, 500 guards of the dockyards, besides fifty guards in the Acropolis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Athenian state established a system where citizens holder specific offices or performing military/judicial duties were entitled to financial support from the imperial revenues.

verbatim: No alteration, however, was made in the method of election of the nine Archons, except that five years after the death of Ephialtes it was decided that the candidates to be submitted to the lot for that office might be selected from the Zeugitae as well as from the higher classes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a legislative change (a decision/decree) that expanded the pool of candidates for the high office of Archon to include the Zeugitae (the third property class), who were previously excluded in favor of the Pentacosiomedimni and Knights.

verbatim: two years afterwards, in the archonship of Antidotus, in consequence of the great increase in the number of citizens, it was resolved, on the motion of Pericles, that no one should be admitted to the franchise who was not of citizen birth by both parents.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the Periclean citizenship law which restricted the resource of political franchise specifically to those with two citizen parents, thereby removing it from those with only one citizen parent.

verbatim: Pericles was also the first to institute pay for service in the law-courts, as a bid for popular favour to counterbalance the wealth of Cimon.
and accordingly he instituted pay for the members of the juries.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text documents a specific rule change introduced by Pericles providing financial compensation for citizens performing the role of jurors, which is a material resource.

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for the Committee of Public Safety
verbatim: The popular Assembly was to elect twenty persons, over forty years of age, who, in conjunction with the existing ten members of the Committee of Public Safety, after taking an oath that they would frame such measures as they thought best for the state, should then prepare proposals for the public safety.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under the oligarchic reforms of the Four Hundred, a rule was established setting a minimum age of forty for the special committee tasked with framing the state's safety measures.

verbatim: All magistrates should serve without remuneration for the period of the war, except the nine Archons and the Prytanes for the time being, who should each receive three obols a day.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a rule during the Coup of 411 BCE where public officials (magistrates) were deprived of their previous right to receive payment for their service, with limited exceptions.

verbatim: The whole of the rest of the administration was to be committed, for the period of the war, to those Athenians who were most capable of serving the state personally or pecuniarily, to the number of not less than five thousand. This body was to have full powers, to the extent even of making treaties with whomsoever they willed
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage outlines a rule where full legislative and administrative power is consolidated into a group defined by their ability to provide financial or military service to the state, effectively excluding the poorer citizens.

verbatim: There should be a Council, holding office for a year, consisting of men over thirty years of age, serving without pay.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific constitutional requirement for the revolutionary Council of Four Hundred/Five Thousand. It establishes an age floor of thirty, excluding younger men from this specific political resource.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to vote in the Assembly
verbatim: Accordingly they deposed the Four Hundred and committed the management of affairs to the Five Thousand, consisting of persons possessing a military equipment.
During this period the constitution of the state seems to have been admirable, since it was a time of war and the franchise was in the hands of those who possessed a military equipment.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the transition from the Four Hundred to the Five Thousand, the 'franchise' (management of affairs) was restricted to those possessing 'military equipment' (the hoplite class). This effectively excludes the lower-class citizens (thetes) who did not own such equipment.

verbatim: At the same time they voted that pay should not be given for any public office.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The abolition of pay for public office is a rule that removes a financial resource (compensation) from citizens. In practice, this serves as a barrier for the poor to participate in governance.

verbatim: as, for instance, by making the testator free once for all to leave his property as he pleased, and abolishing the existing limitations in cases of insanity, old age, and undue female influence, in order that no opening might be left for professional accusers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Thirty Tyrants repealed laws that allowed wills to be challenged on the grounds of 'undue female influence'. By 'freeing' the testator from this limitation, the rule legally diminishes the recognized influence or standing women had in the context of testamentary law.

verbatim: But so soon as they had got a firmer hold on the city, they spared no class of citizens, but put to death any persons who were eminent for wealth or birth or character. Herein they aimed at removing all whom they had reason to fear, while they also wished to lay hands on their possessions
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systematic policy by the Thirty to target 'eminent' individuals for their resources (birth/wealth). This indicates a suspension of the typical protection from arbitrary execution for these specific sub-groups.

verbatim: They introduced two laws into the Council, which they commanded it to pass; the first of them gave the Thirty absolute power to put to death any citizen who was not included in the list of the Three Thousand
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific legal change during the reign of the Thirty where a sub-group of citizens (those not in the 'Three Thousand') lost their legal protection against summary execution by the authorities.

verbatim: the second disqualified all persons from participation in the franchise who should have assisted in the demolition of the fort of Eetioneia, or have acted in any way against the Four Hundred who had organized the previous oligarchy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records a law that stripped specific citizens of their franchise based on past political actions against a previous regime.

verbatim: Theramenes having been thus removed, they disarmed all the people except the Three Thousand, and in every respect showed a great advance in cruelty and crime.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The 'Three Thousand' (a group defined by an enrollment list, effectively a wealth/status tier) were the only ones permitted to keep arms, while the rest of the citizenry had this resource removed by the ruling body.

verbatim: All persons who, having remained in the city during the troubles, were now anxious to leave it, were to be free to settle at Eleusis, retaining their civil rights and possessing full and independent powers of self-government, and with the free enjoyment of their own personal property.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The reconciliation terms established a specific right for the group of citizens who chose to settle in Eleusis to have their own independent administration.

verbatim: No one who settled at Eleusis should be capable of holding any office in Athens until he should again register himself on the roll as a resident in the city.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule explicitly bars citizens living in the seceded community of Eleusis from holding office in the main city of Athens.

verbatim: his subsequent prosecution of Thrasybulus on the charge of illegality, for a motion by which he proposed to confer the franchise on all who had taken part in the return from Piraeus, although some of them were notoriously slaves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The successful prosecution of a motion to grant slaves the franchise proves that the prevailing legal norm in Athens excluded slaves from receiving citizenship rights.

verbatim: At first they refused to allow payment for attendance at the Assembly; but the result was that people did not attend. Consequently, after the Prytanes had tried many devices... Agyrrhius, in the first instance, made a provision of one obol a day... and Agyrrhius again to three.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the established practice of his own time where citizens are paid a specific sum (three obols) to attend political meetings.

verbatim: The franchise is open to all who are of citizen birth by both parents. They are enrolled among the demesmen at the age of eighteen. On the occasion of their enrolment the demesmen give their votes on oath, first whether the candidates appear to be of the age prescribed by the law [...] and secondly whether the candidate is free born and of such parentage as the laws require.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle describes the standard requirement for entering the citizen body in his time. Access to the franchise (political rights) is strictly limited to those who can prove both their age and that both parents were citizens.

verbatim: For these two years they are on garrison duty, and wear the military cloak, and during this time they are exempt from all taxes. They also can neither bring an action at law, nor have one brought against them, in order that they may have no excuse for requiring leave of absence.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Young men during their two years of mandatory military service (Ephebi) are granted specific legal and financial immunities to ensure they focus on their duties.

verbatim: They also can neither bring an action at law, nor have one brought against them, in order that they may have no excuse for requiring leave of absence; though exception is made in cases of actions concerning inheritances and wards of state, or of any sacrificial ceremony connected with the family.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Ephebi (aged 18-20) are protected from being sued or having to participate in lawsuits, preserving their time for garrison duty.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to speak in the Assembly
verbatim: Another Assembly in each prytany is assigned to the hearing of petitions, and at this meeting any one is free, on depositing the petitioner’s olive-branch, to speak to the people concerning any matter, public or private.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the Athenian Assembly described by Aristotle, 'anyone' refers to the citizens who constitute the assembly, granting them a specific right to initiate discussion on private or public grievances.

verbatim: Eumelides of Alopece rescued him from its hands, maintaining that no citizen ought to be put to death except on the decision of a court of law. [...] and the people deprived the Council thenceforward of the power to inflict death or imprisonment or fine, passing a law that if the Council condemn any person for an offence or inflict a fine, the Thesmothetae shall bring the sentence or fine before the law-court.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule following a historical precedent: the Council cannot unilaterally execute citizens; such decisions must be ratified by a law-court, providing a resource of legal protection.

verbatim: Private individuals, also, may lay an information against any magistrate they please for not obeying the laws, but here too there is an appeal to the law-courts if the Council declare the charge proved.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle notes that ordinary citizens have the power to initiate legal actions against magistrates who break the law, which is a significant political resource.

verbatim: According to the law of Solon—which is still in force—they must be Pentacosiomedimni, but in point of fact the person on whom the lot falls holds the office even though he be quite a poor man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Although the formal law restricted high financial offices to the wealthiest class (Pentacosiomedimni), Aristotle clarifies that in contemporary practice, even the poor can hold these offices if selected by lot.

verbatim: they call up the persons newly enrolled, and if any one makes affidavit that he is either physically or pecuniarily incapable of cavalry service they dismiss him, but if no such affidavit is made the Council vote whether the individual in question is suitable for the purpose or not.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a formal legal process where individuals enrolled for the cavalry can be excused if they prove they lack the financial means (pecuniarily incapable) to maintain a horse and equipment.

verbatim: The Council also examines infirm paupers; for there is a law which provides that persons possessing less than three minas, who are so crippled as to be unable to do any work, are, after examination by the Council, to receive two obols a day from the state for their support.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Aristotle cites a specific law providing a material resource (money) to a group defined by health (crippled) and wealth (less than three minas) criteria.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to set market prices for labor
verbatim: Their duty is to see that female flute-and harp-and lute-players are not hired at more than two drachmas, and if more than one person is anxious to hire the same girl, they cast lots and hire her out to the person to whom the lot falls.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The City Commissioners (Astynomi) enforce a price ceiling on the wages these specific workers can earn, limiting their economic freedom.

verbatim: The Arbitrators are persons in the sixtieth year of their age; this appears from the schedule of the Archons and the Eponymi. ... The law enacts that any one who does not serve as Arbitrator when he has arrived at the necessary age shall lose his civil rights, unless he happens to be holding some other office during that year, or to be out of the country.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Men in their 60th year are legally compelled to perform a specific civic duty (arbitration) under penalty of losing their status as citizens.

verbatim: If they detect any magistrate in embezzlement, the jury condemn him for theft, and he is obliged to repay tenfold the sum he is declared to have misappropriated. If they charge a magistrate with accepting bribes and the jury convict him, they fine him for corruption, and this sum too is repaid tenfold.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Those holding the status of 'magistrate' are subjected to specific, heightened financial penalties (tenfold repayment) compared to ordinary citizens for similar financial crimes.

verbatim: When they are examined, they are asked, first, ‘Who is your father, and of what deme? who is your father’s father? who is your mother? who is your mother’s father, and of what deme?’ Then the candidate is asked whether he possesses an ancestral Apollo and a household Zeus, and where their sanctuaries are; next if he possesses a family tomb, and where; then if he treats his parents well, and pays his taxes, and has served on the required military expeditions.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the specific criteria used during the 'dokimasia' (examination) of the nine Archons. It establishes that access to these high magistracies is contingent upon verifying specific lineage, religious adherence, and civic duty.

verbatim: Next he assigns Choregi to the tragic poets, choosing three of the richest persons out of the whole body of Athenians. Formerly he used also to assign five Choregi to the comic poets, but now the tribes provide the Choregi for them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Archon is explicitly tasked with selecting the wealthiest individuals to fund the tragic choruses (Choregi), which is a material burden (liturgy) imposed based on the wealth criterion.

verbatim: he transacts the exchanges of properties for them, and reports any excuses that are tendered, if any one says that he has already borne this burden, or that he is exempt because he has borne a similar burden and the period of his exemption has not yet expired, or that he is not of the required age; since the Choregus of a boys’ chorus must be over forty years of age.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text defines a negative restriction based on age: those under forty are excluded from the specific resource/responsibility of being a Choregus for a boys' chorus.

verbatim: Cases of unintentional homicide, or of intent to kill, or of killing a slave or a resident alien or a foreigner, are heard by the court of Palladium.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific legal venue (the Palladium) for trials involving the killing of a slave, indicating that slaves had a legal protection against being killed, which was enforced by the King Archon.

verbatim: Cases of unintentional homicide, or of intent to kill, or of killing a slave or a resident alien or a foreigner, are heard by the court of Palladium.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Metics (resident aliens) are explicitly mentioned as a group whose killing is prosecuted through a specific court, demonstrating a legal resource of physical protection.

verbatim: Only private actions come before him, namely those in which resident aliens, both ordinary and privileged, and agents of foreign states are concerned.
and in fact, generally, whatever the Archon does for citizens, the Polemarch does for aliens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Polemarch’s primary role is to provide non-citizens with access to the same legal protections and private actions that the Archon provides to citizens, ensuring they have a resource of legal recourse.

verbatim: Moreover they bring up certain private suits in cases of merchandise and mines, or where a slave has slandered a free man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies 'a slave slandering a free man' as a specific legal action brought before the Thesmothetae, indicating that slaves were legally restricted from certain types of speech against the free in-group.

verbatim: Formerly the state used to sell the fruit itself, and if any one dug up or broke down one of the sacred olives, he was tried by the Council of Areopagus, and if he was condemned, the penalty was death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This historical rule shows that owners of land with sacred olives had very restricted rights over those specific trees, with the penalty of death for destroying them until the procedure was modernized.

verbatim: In the first place, ten Generals [Strategi], who were formerly elected one from each tribe, but now are chosen from the whole mass of citizens. Their duties are assigned to them by open vote; one is appointed to command the heavy infantry, and leads them if they go out to war; one to the defence of the country, who remains on the defensive, and fights if there is war within the borders of the country
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a shift from tribal representation to a system where any member of the citizen body is eligible for election as a General, increasing access to high military command.

verbatim: Pay is received for the following services. First the members of the Assembly receive a drachma for the ordinary meetings, and nine obols for the ‘sovereign’ meeting.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author lists the specific material compensation provided to citizens for exercising their political right to attend the Assembly.

verbatim: Then the jurors at the law-courts receive three obols; and the members of the Council five obols. The Prytanes receive an allowance of an obol for their maintenance.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that citizens performing the role of jurors or council members are entitled to daily financial compensation.

verbatim: All persons above thirty years of age are qualified to serve as jurors, provided they are not debtors to the state and have not lost their civil rights.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule explicitly bars those under thirty, those in state debt, and those without full civil rights from the resource of participating in the judicial system.

verbatim: All persons above thirty years of age are qualified to serve as jurors, provided they are not debtors to the state and have not lost their civil rights. If any unqualified person serves as juror, an information is laid against him, and he is brought before the court; and, if he is convicted, the jurors assess the punishment or fine which they consider him to deserve.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Being a debtor to the state (a wealth/property status) results in the loss of the right to sit on a jury, enforceable by legal penalty.

verbatim: The military offices may be held any number of times, but none of the others more than once, except the membership of the Council, which may be held twice.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While most offices are restricted to a single term, military offices are uniquely exempted, allowing citizens more access to these specific positions of power over time.

verbatim: Whichever has the majority is victorious; but if the votes are equal the verdict is for the defendant.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes the standard operating procedure of the Athenian courts during his time. In the event of a tie in the jury's ballot, the rule mandates a result in favor of the defendant, granting them a specific legal protection against conviction.

verbatim: Finally, when all has been completed in accordance with the law, the jurors receive their pay in the order assigned by the lot.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the standard conclusion of a court session where the individuals acting as jurors are entitled to material compensation (pay) for their service to the state.

verbatim: After four Archons had ruled on these conditions, the office was thrown open to all the Eupatridae, or nobles; and in 682 B.C. the board of nine annual Archons was substituted for the decennial Archon.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The historical footnote describes how the highest political office (the archonship) was specifically reserved for the class of nobles (Eupatridae) following the transition from the royal house. This defines access to political power based on nobility/lineage.

verbatim: The statue set up by Anthemion must have been his own, not his father's, since the latter, as the inscription proves, could not properly have been represented with a horse, as he was only a member of the Thetes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text and accompanying footnote discuss the census classes (Pentacosiomedimni, Knights, Zeugites, and Thetes). The Thetes, being the poorest class, were historically excluded from the higher magistracies like the archonship, which required a higher wealth qualification.

verbatim: The archonship of Mnesitheides was in 457 B.C.; and as the death of Ephialtes was in 462 B.C., and it has just been stated that the alteration in the law was made five years later, it follows that a Zeugites was elected for the first year in which the members of that order were eligible.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text records a specific legal change in 457 B.C. where the Zeugites (the third wealth class in Athens) were granted the right to hold the office of Archon, a role previously restricted to higher wealth tiers.

verbatim: It is clear from many allusions in the Ecclesiazusae of Aristophanes that the rate of pay had been raised to three obols shortly before the performance of that play in 392 B.C.; and the first establishment of payment for attendance at the Assembly cannot be placed many years before that date.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the establishment and subsequent increase of monetary compensation for citizens participating in the legislative assembly (Ecclesia).

verbatim: i.e. whether he is born of two citizen parents.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The note verifies the requirement that for an individual to be a valid citizen, both parents must be citizens, effectively restricting access to the group based on lineage.

verbatim: When a man died leaving a daughter, but no son, his estate, though not becoming her property, was attached to her, and the nearest of kin could claim her in marriage; and the property went to the sons born of such marriage.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the institution of the epikleros, where a daughter without brothers cannot own her father's estate; instead, she is 'attached' to the estate and must marry a kinsman so the property passes to her male offspring.

extracted_rules.rule: Kinship dowry obligation for poor heiresses
verbatim: If she was poor, the nearest of kin was obliged either to marry her or to provide her with a dowry. If there were more daughters than one, the estate seems to have been divided among them under similar conditions. These heiresses were under the special protection of the Archon.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule mandates that the nearest male relative must provide a dowry for a fatherless daughter if she is poor and he chooses not to marry her, ensuring her financial support through the Archon's oversight.

verbatim: The trierarchs were the persons (chosen from the richest men in the community) who were required to undertake the equipment of a trireme at their own expense. Like the office of Choregus (ch. 56, 3, 4) it was a public duty performed by private individuals.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the specific obligation (trierarchy and choregia) imposed on the wealthiest members of the community to fund naval equipment and public festivals at their own expense.

verbatim: Every person who had held any public office had to submit himself and his accounts to examination before a jury at the end of his term of office; on which occasion any citizen might impeach his conduct during his office.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule where the general body of citizens has the right to challenge or impeach any official during their end-of-term audit.

verbatim: The state still continued its protection of heiresses even after they were married. Its care only ceased when they had children capable of inheriting the property.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes a protective legal status afforded to women who are sole heiresses (epikleroi), ensuring state oversight of their welfare and property until an heir is born.

verbatim: Every alien resident in Athens was required to provide himself with a patron from among the citizens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Non-citizens (meticos/aliens) were legally required to have a citizen sponsor (prostate) to navigate the legal system, representing a lack of independent legal standing.

verbatim: During the last year of his cycle, however, he was required to serve not as a soldier but as an Arbitrator; and accordingly each year the Forty took the list of those who were commencing their last year of service, and assigned to them the duties which they were to undertake as arbitrators during the year.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Athenian men in their final year of military eligibility (age 59) were legally compelled to serve as public arbitrators.

verbatim: If any person considered that he had been unduly saddled with one of the burdens which rich men were called upon to bear for the state (such as the equipment of a chorus or a trireme), he might require any one on whom he thought the burden should rather have been laid either to undertake it, or else to submit to an exchange of properties.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law allowed a wealthy person to challenge their selection for a liturgy by identifying a wealthier individual to either take the burden or trade all assets.
